data.text:
INT: Tape 6 of Mark Verstandig. In finishing off the section about your schooling, secondary schooling that is, how many Jewish students would there have been in your year in the class? 

MV: In the class would have been four or five, a small percentage. 

INT: Out of how, how many total? 

MV: Well, there would have been about 10%, so-- well, less than 50%. 

INT: Was there any-- 

MV: There were not many-- 

INT: Was there-- 

MV: --not many Jews. 

INT: Was there any ethnic tension at all? 

MV: No, not at all. Not at all. Not at all. 

It is my-- still my belief that without outside agitation and incitement by a few, there wouldn't have been any problem. 

INT: And when you finished gymnasium, what did you go on to do? 

MV: When I finished gymnasium, I went to Cracow to study law. And I enrolled. But then a law was passed by the government to increase the fees, let us say from $20-- from 20 zlotys, so even less, to 350 zlotys per annum. 

That was a-- that was at that time a large amount of money. I could not afford it. My father didn't have it. It was the time of the depression. 

INT: What year could that have been? 

MV: Would have been '29, '28, from '28 to '29. That was the-- the rural depression started in Poland already year before. In America, it crashed in 1929. But in Poland, the rural depression-- but I didn't have the money. 

See, because-- you see, it started in, in October, the, the university year started in October in Poland. And you had to pay at the beginning of, of '29. It was a lot in the deep. It was a lot in the depression. We didn't have the money. 

Everybody knew that this increase in fees was designed to push out the Jewish students from the university, because at the same time, there was a regulation that if one could supply a-- not an-- a-- if one could supply some kind of a document stating that the parents of this student are not able to pay the money, they could be free altogether. 

The catch was that, that people who are signing such documents-- they're called [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's not a poverty, but it was an inability of paying-- that this document was-- had to be issued by the city council or the village council. And non-Jewish students had no difficulty in getting such a document that the parents are not able to sign-- to-- to pay the $350. Whereas, Jews were normally-- were normally considered rich people. 

There was one case-- I mean, there was a case that a Jew in a-- in a little town, in [NON-ENGLISH] or where I don't remember, in, in a little town not far from the Russian border. I remember this case because it was-- it was mentioned in Parliament in the same-- it was in the same mentioned by a Jewish deputy, called-- I don't remember him. It was a-- he was a representative of the Mizrahi. I'll remember in a minute his name. 

He-- he-- he brought up this method, that the fellow peddler, a hawker, was a who-- who-- a fellow hawker was given a tax assessment of 100,000 zlotys. This whole village where this lived-- where this fellow's whole little, this whole little shtetl, all the-- all, all the-- all the little sheds together with the size, you know, with-- were not worth half the money. It, it was at that time-- it was at that time a kind of a forcing Jews to emigrate. 

So they're forcing Jews to emigrate in a way that they are putting-- that they are issuing assessment to pay taxes out of the air, 100,000 zlotys, 20,000. This Jew didn't have in his-- didn't, didn't have any-- in his capital even 100 zlotys, not, not only 100,000 zlotys. 

And a lot of Jews at that time left Poland. They went to France. They went to Germany. And they went to Palestine. But they left. 

And that was a case. So it was impossible to get-- for a Jewish student to get a-- oh, to get a document of hardship. That was the expression, a document of hardship, which would relieve him from paying 350 zlotys, entry fee 350 zlotys. And therefore, the Jews will disappear from the universities. 

I was very distressed. My father didn't have the money. And I went home. I asked him. And he told me, look, what can I do? 

He was-- he was lying in bed. He had a cold. It would have been the night. 

And he, he told me, look, take my fur coat to Cracow. Sell it. And you will have enough money for the 350 zlotys and to live on for a year or two, 'cause it was a very expensive fur coat. 

But I-- I-- I didn't-- I couldn't accept it, because this fur coat was the last sign of prestige for my father. Without his fur coat, he would-- he would die. You know, a fur coat was his kind of-- of-- of status. I couldn't have done that. 

I went to back to Cracow. And we were-- I was waiting for a miracle, because he was going on, what was-- what-- what will happen? Anyhow, it so happened that I had that-- a-- a kind of a-- a kind of an organization called B'nai B'rith, a secret organization called. But I never heard of this organization. 

Like the, like the Freemasons, that they have got a fund. They establish a fund. If there is real hardship, they will pay the 350 zlotys. 

But I didn't have a chance, because I was the son of a landowner. I was a rich man. So I didn't have a chance. 

But I went to a friend of mine, a certain Leon Selpeter. He had a-- he was a big shot in Cracow. He comes from-- he came from Mielec. His brother was a famous lawyer. And he was the brother-in-law of my-- my wife now, but at that time of my fiancee. 

And I knew this Selpeter from, from childhood. He was a colleague of my brother, this Leon Selpeter in Cracow, who was a-- was a pharmacist. He was the president of the Zionist organization, not only for Galicia. But he was the president for Galicia and the vice president of the- of the so-called-- the so-called Zionist B organization, Birthright. 

I went to him. And he fixed it. He fixed it. 

But before I knew [INAUDIBLE], before I knew that he fixed it-- because he had to fix it in such a way that the Jewish student organization shouldn't know about it. Because the Jewish student organization had to submit a list to the B'nai B'rith. Who was not on their list shouldn't have got it. And then I said-- I told him, when everything came together, three, three miracles came together. That is Leon Selpeter fixed, fixed the fee, that they are going to pay the fee. 

At the same time, on a-- I think on a Shabbat, on a Shabbat at my-- at my cousin's place, who was good lawyer in Cracow, my auntie's place, who had one son only. We were sitting there on a Saturday and listening to music over the radio. We heard an announcement about the tickets, about the lottery ticket. And I won a lottery ticket together with a friend of mine from another town, a friend of mine who lives next door to me in this house, in this academic house, in the hostel. This is called hostel, yeah, Jewish hostel. 

We won 1,000 zlotys. And 1,000 zlotys was a hell of a lot of money. I won 500 zlotys. 

And a few days later, Professor [NON-ENGLISH] called me in. I knew he was a famous professor of Roman law. And one day, he lectured. And he gave to-- he, he explained that the Roman law introduced, introduced the differentiation between interpretation of a document inter vivos-- between people alive-- and a testament, because between people alive, a document, the magistrate has to-- a magistrate has to interpret the document as it is written there. He has to try to understand what is written there, and this is how it should be interpreted. 

Whereas in a testament, the magistrate has to try to establish what was the will of the testator, of the man who wrote the testament. He has to go into, into his-- into-- into-- into-- into his will, what he meant by that, not what is written, but what he really meant by that. He explained it. And that was, he said, the great innovation in Roman law, which was accepted later by all-- by-- by-- by all-- by the jurisprudence all over the world. 

After the lecture, I went over to him. I told him, look, it's a nice-- it's a nice-- a nice way. It is this is how it should be. But it-- they were not the first one. 

The Jewish law, the Talmud provided for it before that. The Talmud said that you should interpret the will, [NON-ENGLISH], to fulfill-- the right-- to fulfill what the-- what the testator, the-- the-- the dead man wanted to say. 

He says, come, come with me. I came, I came to his office. He told me, look, I had rabbis here, and they never told me this. I said, maybe they had a great respect for you, as I have, but they didn't dare. And I have got a bit of chutzpah, so I am telling you, this is-- this is this is Jewish law. 

Wait, I've got something for you. He took from a-- from the shelf a book written by Asher Gulak, Professor Asher Gulak in Hebrew. There are four books, four-- four volumes. He gave me the first volume. 

He said, this is-- Asher Gulak is a friend of mine. And he-- somehow he-- he codified the Jewish Talmudic law in the way Gaius did in Roman law. Do the first chapter, the personal laws, the personal Jewish law, and compare with Gaius. Compare it with the Roman law. And I will pay you for it. 

I did it. And it took me a bit of time. But I-- and they-- he paid me nicely. That is true. He paid me nicely. 

I had the impression that he didn't need it. He needed this it himself, not for me. He needed it, because he couldn't read it. Anyhow, this is how it looked. 

Now, what happened is I'm telling you that just a few days after this winning the lottery and having the fees paid by B'nai B'rith, he called me in. I went to his lecture-- his lectures. And after the lecture, he called me in. And I thought certainly he is going to give me another chapter and to tell me that when I finish the other chapter, he will give me some money. 

But when I came in, he gave me a note to the bursar-- for the bursar for 50 zlotys or for 100 zlotys-- and to me for 100, very nice sum of money. He said, I was very pleased with that. And here you go, and-- and-- and get your money. And here is you have to do another chapter. 

And that is how I started with this, which ended up in a dissertation of-- from Asher Gulak-- from Asher Gulak to Gaius. Now that was- that- and the university. It was not an easy life. It was very bad, because wherever I came, I was the son of a Jewish landowner-- of a Jewish-- of-- of a-- of an owner of a Jewish estate, of-- of-- of a land estate, a very rich man. 

I didn't have a penny. And I had to chase-- I had to chase money. I was running around like mad the whole day. I was running around with-- with tuitions, giving-- instructing students, these high school students with their work. 

And I couldn't afford even to run-- I couldn't afford a train ticket. So I had to-- I had to march from one-- from one place to another place. I came home very late. 

I was active in the Revisionist organization, the Betar-- in the Betar. In the end, I did not attend even the-- I attended only the lectures of [NON-ENGLISH], because he was a great lecturer and a great scholar. I attended the lectures of Professor Kutcheba. He studied Polish history-- well, the Polish-- he-- the-- the old Polish laws. 

Again, because he was a great scholar and a-- an excellent lecturer, a great scholar-- and he was the author of a book about Polish laws pertaining to Jews from the-- the last 1,000 years, from-- from A to-- from-- from the-- from the-- from the time of [NON-ENGLISH]-- how the Jews came in and then they wanted the cemetery and so on and so on and so on. 

Now, this is-- to the other I didn't attend. It was not necessary, because the exams were oral exams at the end of the year. And you could buy this-- you could buy on the black market the lectures of all-- of all-- of all professors who were-- of all subjects. There were even some-- there were even some subjects where you could buy, on the black market, the questions and answers, short questions. And if you go through that one night, you make the-- you-- you pass the exam. 

So I didn't-- I didn't attend. I had my own business. I had business to attend. I was running around. I was running around from day to day, from the whole night. I came back sometimes 10 o'clock, 8 o'clock at night. 

And I was reading till 2 o'clock. I was reading late at night and getting up late in the morning, running down, rushing down to the next-- to the kiosk, where I used to borrow the paper, the daily paper, the Moment and the Haynt, the Yiddish papers. Not the Courier, because the Courier one could read in-- in-- in the-- in the library, in-- in-- in-- where there was the Jewish academic house, the hostel, there was a library over there. You could read it there, though I used to borrow from him the Haynt and the Moment. 

And on Friday, I used to borrow-- Friday was a very-- was a very good paper, a lot of articles of the best Jewish writers in Moment and the Haynt. And I used to pay him a few pence a week and borrow the paper and give it back-- give it back to him. 

Now, this is how I started in the morning. On the way back, I used to buy-- have a-- a bottle of milk. And I had this chocolate. I used to mix it with this cacao-- and a piece of bread. And that was how I lived. 

I didn't have money to go for it. With all the-- with all the work done, I didn't have money to go to a proper dinner, to have a proper dinner. This is how-- but the moment I won the-- I won the lottery, I became a rich man. 

I went up. I bought a pair of shoes, new shoes, and to re-sole the older ones. I bought-- it was winter. I bought a nice-- a nice wool coat and a nice hat. And I bought two, two tennis rackets. 

And there was snow all over. And it was the middle of winter, maybe minus 20 degrees. And with the two rackets, I bought a return ticket to Mielec to give one racket to my-- to my fiancee. It was-- that was the wealthy year. 

INT: How many years did you attend university for? 

MV: Beg pardon? 

INT: How many years did you go to university? 

MV: Four. 

INT: Four years. Were Jewish students a very small minority? 

MV: I wouldn't say they were a small minority. But they were a minority. They were a minority. 

And again, the-- it was not an easy-- it was a not-- not an easy life at the-- at the university. As far as the professors are concerned, was no antisemitism. I never-- I could-- I-- I could never trace any antisemitic word from a professor. They were very nice to everybody. 

But the student organizations, you see, they were organized-- there was an organization by the [NON-ENGLISH] and later by the falangists, [NON-ENGLISH]. 

And they organized almost pogroms every year, because, unlike the university in Australia, a policeman could not enter the campus of the university unless called in by the vice chancellor. 

And there they were-- they were kicking. And they, they were beating up Jews. Every year that-- the year started beating up Jews, Jewish students, and marching and there was anti-Jewish slogans. 

And I had a brush-- the second year, I had a brush with them. I was sent out by the Jewish student organization in the-- the-- a Jewish academic house. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. They sent me to have a look what's going on. There was going-- there was-- in one of the halls was a meeting of the [NON-ENGLISH], of the [NON-ENGLISH] to see what's going on there, because from time to time, they organized marches against the Jewish academic house, which was in the Jewish quarter. 

So I went over there. I listened fine. I listened to everything. And then I realized that they are going to march to the Jewish academic house. 

I said, it's time to telephone them to be prepared. So I stood up a couple of minutes too early to walk off. And then somebody must have recognized me. Like a pack of wolves they came on me. 

And I don't know what happened. But I found myself a flight of stairs down, lying in blood. They-- it was-- I was-- they hit me. They hit me and kicked me. Really, I-- I-- I was-- I was almost half killed. 

Somebody recognized me. That was all the time. But I didn't go in anymore to such meetings. 

As a matter of fact, I remember a-- a couple of years later, there was a kind of a-- of an alignment between the Jewish students and the [NON-ENGLISH] students, it means the [NON-ENGLISH] students against the [NON-ENGLISH], because the [NON-ENGLISH] were in power in the student union-- was in that-- in the student union, using this expression. They had these libraries and everything. And they-- they were selling on black market. They were selling the-- the-- old-- old lectures. 

Anyhow, there was an alignment between the Jewish students and them. And I still remember a fellow called Bierszetczki, Borislav Bierszetczki, from the falangists. He came along to try to convince the other students, the [NON-ENGLISH] students, the Szalasi students, to to abandon-- to split them from the Jews and to go against the Jews and so on. 

And I was very impressed with this fellow. He was an excellent-- an excellent debater, an excellent speaker. He didn't succeed. 

But although he didn't succeed, he worked very hard. And I was very impressed. [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah. I, I was very impressed with him. 

And later, you know, this was this Borislav Bierszetczki, who in 1937 said about-- about-- about Mussolini that Mussolini is naive, that he should take-- he should take the-- he should take the lesson from Hitler how to deal with the Jews, that there is no possibility of having any alliance with Jews, because Mussolini had some, some Jews in his parliament. He said, Mussolini doesn't understand the Jewish question. He should follow Hitler. 

That is the Bierszetczki. And that is the Bierszetczki who, after the war, when the Communists came in, he was against the [NON-ENGLISH]. He was-- he was one of the top men there. 

INT: Thank you. We'll continue next tape.
